content.problemStatement:
Given a binary search tree and an integer SSS, check if there exist two elements in the BST such that their sum is equal to SSS.

For eg., in the following BST, for given S = 8, there exists few pairs of nodes whose sum is 8:




Input Format

The first line of the input contains two space separated integers NNN and SSS — the number of nodes in the binary search tree, and the target sum.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Print `YES`, if there exists at least one such pair of nodes, whose sum is equal to SSS, else `NO`.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two space‑separated integers N and S, where N is the number of nodes in the BST and S is the target sum.
- The next N‑1 lines each contain three space‑separated items: pi ci Ri. pi is the value of the parent node, ci is the value of the child node, and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi). All node values are distinct.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exist two distinct nodes whose values sum to S, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: There exist pairs of nodes in the binary search tree whose sum is equal to the target sum.
There exist pairs of nodes in the binary search tree whose sum is equal to the target sum.
There do not exist pairs of nodes in the binary search tree whose sum is equal to the target sum.

solution.approach: For each test case we first reconstruct the BST from the given edges. While building we keep track of every value that appears as a child; the node that never appears as a child is the root. After the tree is built we perform a depth‑first traversal. During the traversal we store all visited node values in an unordered hash set. For the current node with value v we check whether (S‑v) already exists in the set – if it does, a required pair has been found and we can stop. If the whole tree is visited without finding such a complement, the answer is NO. The hash‑set lookup is O(1) on average, so the whole algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(N) extra space for the set and the tree nodes.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the BST.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the tree nodes and the hash set.
